skill: Inference from sample statistics and margin of error

aspects.name: Scale sample to population estimate
aspects.description: Use a sample proportion, percentage, or mean to estimate a count or total for the larger population. Requires multiplicative scaling (proportion or mean times population/total size), with no use of a margin of error to build an interval.
aspects.boundary_with_other_aspects: A1 produces a single point estimate (or chooses a single value that matches a scale-up calculation). A2 produces a plausible-value interval using a stated margin of error. A1 questions never give a margin of error; A2 questions always do.

types.name: Sample percent/proportion to population count
types.description: Stem gives a population size and a sample percent or fraction with a certain attribute; asks for the estimated number in the full population. Solution = (sample proportion) x (population size).
types.core_skill_demand: Apply proportional reasoning: convert a sample percent or count-ratio into a proportion and multiply by the total population to produce a count estimate.

characteristics: round-friendly numbers (e.g., 30% of 50; 80% of 400; 20% of 55)
single multiplication step
population size is small or a clean multiple
answer choices clearly separate sample count vs. scaled-up count
easy.example_stem: A city has 50 council members. 6 of 20 polled supported a bill. Estimate how many of the 50 support it.

characteristics: non-round population (e.g., 14,878) or non-round sample fraction (e.g., 7/50)
answer choices are 'best estimate' ranges or numerical values requiring rounding
distractors include sample-only count and 'must have' vs 'best estimate' wording trap
medium.example_stem: Of 80 random receipts, 20 showed fruit purchase. Of 1,500 customers, best estimate of fruit-buyers?

characteristics: answer requires a follow-up operation after the scale-up (e.g., difference between two scaled estimates, complement of an attribute)
non-trivial ratio (e.g., 483/803) requiring computation
distractors include sample-level difference and raw scaled count without follow-up
hard.example_stem: 483 of 803 polled voters chose A; the rest chose B. If 6,424 vote, by how many votes does A win?

must_preserve: explicit random sample drawn from a stated total population
single scale-up multiplication is the core operation
no margin of error appears in the stem
must_vary: whether the sample proportion is given as percent, decimal, or count/count
common_bugs: adding a margin of error and turning this into an A2 interval question (categorical drift)
using 'must equal' wording instead of 'best estimate' (changes correct answer set)
answer choice for sample-count and scaled-count too close together, making distractor trivial

types.name: Sample mean to population total
types.description: Sample gives a mean per unit (e.g., trees per acre, items per box) over a small random sample; asks for an estimate of the total or for the population mean times population size.
types.core_skill_demand: Compute the sample mean from a list, then multiply by the population size (or compare to a target range).

characteristics: small list of integer sample values that average to an integer
answer choices are wide non-overlapping ranges
distractors built from using min, median, or max of sample instead of mean
easy.example_stem: Sample of 5 acres has tree counts 51, 59, 45, 52, 73. Forest is 253 acres. Which range contains best estimate?

characteristics: requires reading a two-way table to obtain the sample count or mean
free-response (SPR) rather than MCQ, no distractors to anchor on
more than one row/column must be combined
medium.example_stem: Table shows 400 polled by sex; estimate voters for Candidate A in a town of 6,000.

characteristics: multi-step scaling: per-class mean times classes times students-per-class, with a 'fewer than / at least' complement
embedded percentage of a non-divisible class size (e.g., 34.6% of 26 → must round to integer count)
hard.example_stem: 34.6% of 26 students have at least 2 siblings; 1,800 classes of average size 26; estimate students with fewer than 2 siblings.

must_preserve: sample-level statistic must be computed (mean of a list, or via a table), not given outright
must_vary: presentation: explicit list vs. two-way table
whether attribute or its complement is asked
common_bugs: simplifying the list so the mean is already given (collapses to T1)
omitting distractors built on min/median/max (loses Easy lever)
forgetting the rounding step in hard variant

aspects.name: Interpret margin of error
aspects.description: Given an estimated statistic and an associated margin of error, choose the conclusion that correctly describes the plausible-value interval for the population parameter. The cognitive operation is to add and subtract the margin to the estimate and select the conclusion phrased as 'plausible values are between (estimate - MoE) and (estimate + MoE)'.
aspects.boundary_with_other_aspects: A2 always provides an explicit margin of error and centers on building or interpreting the (estimate ± MoE) interval. A1 has no margin of error and produces a point estimate. A3 turns on the *population/subgroup* the claim applies to, not on the interval calculation.

types.name: Build plausible-value interval for parameter
types.description: Stem gives an estimated population parameter (proportion, percent, or mean) and a margin of error, and asks for the best interpretation. Correct choice is 'plausible values are between (estimate - MoE) and (estimate + MoE).'
types.core_skill_demand: Identify that the population parameter (not individual values, not the sample) lies in [estimate - MoE, estimate + MoE], and reject distractors that (a) make the parameter exact, (b) claim individuals lie in the interval, or (c) place the parameter outside the interval.

characteristics: integer or one-decimal estimate and MoE (e.g., 37 ± 3; 38% ± 5.5%)
bound arithmetic is one-step mental math
wrong choices are obviously different (less-than vs greater-than vs interval)
exactly one option states the correct interval
easy.example_stem: Random sample mean is 37 with margin of error 3. Best conclusion about population mean?

characteristics: distractors swap 'mean of population' with 'every value', 'sample', or 'not possible outside'
one distractor uses 'not possible' / 'every value' to test conceptual scope, not arithmetic
medium.example_stem: Estimated proportion 0.49, MoE 0.04. Most appropriate conclusion about population proportion?

characteristics: interval is implicit; student must evaluate a specific outside-interval claim ('is 35% plausible?')
answer is phrased as 'it is doubtful' / 'unlikely' rather than the literal bounds
distractors include certainty claims ('not possible', 'exactly')
hard.example_stem: Estimate 30% with MoE 3%; is the claim that 35% are heavy texters correct? Pick the appropriate conclusion.

must_preserve: explicit margin of error in stem
exactly one option states the correct (estimate - MoE, estimate + MoE) bounds for the population parameter
at least one distractor confuses sample with population, individual with mean, or 'not possible outside' with 'less plausible outside'
must_vary: parameter type (mean, percent, proportion) and units
magnitude and decimal precision of estimate and MoE
common_bugs: cloning with MoE that produces a negative or impossible lower bound (e.g., 5% ± 8%)
writing all distractors as arithmetic variants, removing the conceptual 'every value / sample / not possible' distractor and making the question trivially mechanical
swapping 'mean' for 'value' in the correct option, accidentally flipping the answer

types.name: Apply MoE to scaled population count
types.description: Stem gives a percent estimate with a margin of error AND a total population, asks for a plausible total count, or for an inequality on the unknown count r. Requires both scaling (A1-like) and interval-building (T3-like).
types.core_skill_demand: Compute (estimate - MoE) x N and (estimate + MoE) x N to bracket the count, then pick the option whose number or inequality falls in the resulting count interval.

characteristics: round percent and MoE; population is a clean multiple
only one option falls in the (low, high) count range; others are far off
easy.example_stem: 10,000 beads; 15% ± 2% estimated red. Which is plausible for actual red count r?

characteristics: answer choices are concrete count values; distractors include sample-only counts and totals outside the scaled interval
MoE is a non-round percent (e.g., 3%) on a small or large population
medium.example_stem: Population 50,000; 35% support with MoE 3%. Which is a plausible total supporting count?

characteristics: answers expressed as inequalities on a variable r, not as a single integer
uses both lower and upper bounds and asks which compound inequality is most plausible
hard.example_stem: If r is actual red bead count and 15% ± 2% of 10,000 are red, which inequality is most plausible?

must_preserve: both a margin of error and a total population size in the stem
answer options refer to counts (or inequalities on counts), not percentages
the correct option lies inside [(p - MoE) x N, (p + MoE) x N]
must_vary: whether choices are inequalities on a variable or numerical counts
common_bugs: scaling only the estimate and not the MoE (turns problem into A1)
producing answer choices where two options both fall inside the bracket
off-by-one in computing (p - MoE) x N for non-round numbers

types.name: Reason about cause of MoE difference
types.description: Two samples drawn from the same population yield different margins of error; stem asks the reason. Tests conceptual knowledge that smaller sample size yields larger margin of error.
types.core_skill_demand: Recall that margin of error decreases as sample size increases (and is essentially independent of the sample percent/response distribution within typical SAT framing); reject distractors based on response rate or favorability.

characteristics: sample sizes are clearly stated and very different
only one option mentions sample size
easy.example_stem: Sample A's MoE is larger than B's. Why? (Choice: A had smaller sample size.)

characteristics: sample sizes given in a table, must be read first
distractors include unrecorded-vote count and favorability percent
medium.example_stem: Two random samples for the same proposition; MoE for A > MoE for B. Best reason?

characteristics: sample-size comparison hidden in a multi-column table
multiple plausible-sounding distractors invoking response rate, favorability, or non-response
requires distinguishing factors that affect MoE from factors that do not
hard.example_stem: Two random samples, same method; why is MoE_A > MoE_B? (Correct: smaller sample size.)

must_preserve: two samples from the same population with the same MoE method
exactly one option attributes the difference to sample size
distractors include at least one plausible-but-incorrect cause (response rate, favorability, etc.)
common_bugs: making the sample-size answer trivially obvious by stating sizes directly in stem instead of in a table
writing distractors that are factually false rather than plausible-but-wrong, removing the discrimination

aspects.name: Identify valid scope of inference
aspects.description: Given a sample drawn from a specific subgroup, identify the population to which the conclusion can validly extend. The cognitive operation is recognizing that the sample only supports inference about the subgroup it was drawn from, not the entire broader population.
aspects.boundary_with_other_aspects: A3 turns on *which population* a conclusion applies to. A1/A2 take the population as given and focus on arithmetic or interval interpretation. A clone that drops the subgroup framing and reverts to a generic-population stem is a categorical drift to A2/T3.

types.name: Restrict conclusion to sampled subgroup
types.description: Sample is drawn from a subgroup (e.g., one species of fish, one club) within a larger population. Stem asks which conclusion is best supported; correct answer restricts the conclusion to the subgroup, while distractors over-generalize to the whole population.
types.core_skill_demand: Distinguish the population actually sampled (subgroup) from a larger population that contains it; recognize that sample statistics support inference only about the sampled subgroup.

characteristics: subgroup is obviously different from the population
only one option restricts to the subgroup
distractors are clearly broader claims
easy.example_stem: Sample of largemouth bass shows 30% over 2 lbs. Best conclusion about pond fish?

characteristics: two distractors over-generalize: one to all fish proportion, one to all fish average
one distractor restates the same fact about the sample without inference language
subgroup is named in the stem only once
medium.example_stem: Sample of 150 largemouth bass; 30% over 2 lb. Best supported conclusion?

characteristics: subgroup is one of several mentioned in the stem; reader must track which one was actually sampled
distractors involve mixing two subgroups or projecting onto an unsampled subgroup
hard.example_stem: Sample drawn from largemouth bass only; which inference about other species or all fish is supported?

must_preserve: sample is explicitly drawn from a named subgroup of a larger population
correct answer restricts inference to the subgroup
at least one distractor over-generalizes from the subgroup to the whole population
common_bugs: removing the subgroup-vs-whole distinction; clone becomes a simple proportion question (drifts to A1/T1)
writing the correct answer with weaker phrasing than at least one distractor, making the over-generalization tempting

skill_wide_notes: All originals frame a 'random sample' explicitly. Two cognitive families dominate: scale-up (A1, no MoE) and interval-interpretation (A2, MoE given). Difficulty within A2/T3 is driven mostly by decimal precision of estimate and MoE plus the conceptual subtlety of distractors (sample vs population, mean vs every value, plausible vs not-possible). Hard A2 items shift from 'compute the bounds' to 'evaluate a specific claim against the implied bounds'. T6 (scope) is rare but distinguished by the subgroup framing.
